headline: Generals charged at last — but the 4,679-contract cartel system remains intact
context: Former Army Chief Hafizuddeain Jantan and former Defence Forces Chief Mohamad Nizam Jaafar face corruption charges. MACC's Ops Parasit seized RM52M. A 26-firm cartel ran 4,679 contracts while just 12 auditors oversaw the entire defence portfolio.

cards.big: Two former military chiefs charged with corruption — Malaysia's first prosecution of its top brass
cards.sub: Hafizuddeain faces money laundering; Nizam faces abuse of power, CBT and receipt of a valuable item. His wife was charged. MACC seized RM52M. An external activist triggered the probe.

cards.big: A 26-firm cartel ran 4,679 contracts while 12 auditors watched — an external activist triggered the probe
cards.sub: The Whistleblower Act excludes OSA material. Military procurement is classified by default. Defence at 1.1% of GDP means corruption takes an outsized bite of a thin budget.

cards.big: The LCS scandal ended in DNAA. Then the same procurement system produced a new cartel on this government's watch
cards.sub: RM6.1B spent on warships that don't exist. Contracts above RM500K required MOF approval — civilian bureaucrats either colluded or failed.

cards.big: The King warned the Ministry was 'full of agents' — constitutionally grounded but potentially prejudicial to fair trial
cards.sub: As Supreme Commander under Article 41, the Agong has a legitimate role. But 'hunt' language on active cases risks defence challenges to fair trial.

cards.big: These are not the first generals caught — they are the first charged. The system that enabled a 4,679-contract cartel has been announced reformed but not actually changed
The prosecution is historic. But soldiers in substandard barracks while chiefs hoarded gold deserve more than theatre — they deserve a system that cannot be captured.
